source.title: Weekly POC check-in — experiment results + guardrails (Northbridge)
source.summary: Northbridge walked through their first 48 hours of route-level experiment traffic. They are seeing expected cost reductions in variant B but are confused by several Console dashboard behaviors (time window alignment, baseline comparison, and what triggers a guardrail badge). Redwood clarified current semantics (soft-stop defaults, guardrail evaluation windows, known propagation lag and attribution drop issues) and agreed on changes: clearer UI copy/tooltips for deltas, making the evaluation window explicit, and tightening guidance around stickiness keys and streaming retry attribution. Next steps include Redwood validating attribution integrity for their route, suggesting tuned thresholds for p99 latency and error rate, and a short enablement note on how to interpret variant headers and results freshness.
source.topics: POC status update; interpreting early A/B results; p50/p95/p99 latency deltas; cost/token math; results dashboard clarity (baseline + time windows); guardrail tuning (soft-stop vs hard-stop, thresholds, evaluation windows); attribution drop rate + propagation lag; variant headers + stickiness key usage; next-week plan (ramp split + quality eval hook readiness).
next_steps: Redwood (Logan) to run attribution integrity checks for route_id=chat_completions_v2 on Northbridge account and report any drop/skew by variant.
Redwood (Rafael) to propose initial guardrail thresholds for Northbridge: p99 latency regression, error rate, and cost/token; confirm soft-stop behavior for POC.
Redwood (Felix) to follow up with screenshots + explanation of dashboard deltas (baseline selection, time window, and results freshness).
Northbridge to confirm the stickiness key they want to standardize on (customer_id vs end_user_id) and verify it is present on streaming retries.
Schedule a 30-min follow-up next week after ramping traffic to 20/80 and collecting a full weekday of data.
action_items: Owner: Logan Wright — Pull a breakdown of experiment attribution drop rate for Northbridge route (by region + streaming vs non-streaming) and share by EOD Thu.
Owner: Rafael Mendes — Send a recommended guardrail template (thresholds + evaluation window) tailored to Northbridge current baseline by Fri.
Owner: Felix Schneider — Draft UI clarification notes (time window, baseline, guardrail badge logic) and share with Northbridge + Marissa by Wed.
Owner: Dylan Brooks — Email Northbridge the short troubleshooting blurb on variant headers and how to confirm assigned variant in logs by Wed.
Owner: Ava Thompson (Northbridge) — Confirm stickiness key source and ensure it is stable for both standard and streaming requests by next check-in.
source.transcript:
Fireflies.ai — Transcript

Meeting: Weekly POC check-in — experiment results + guardrails (Northbridge)
Date/Time: 2025-03-04 10:00 AM (PT)
Duration: 43 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Dylan Brooks, Felix Schneider, Logan Wright, Rafael Mendes
Northbridge: Ava Thompson, Mark Feldman, Priya Shah

---

[00:00] Dylan Brooks: Alright, cool. I think we’re recording. Ava, Mark, Priya — good to see you all. Today we wanted to do the weekly check-in, look at early results from the route experiment, and then also talk through guardrails. Felix and Logan are on too.

[00:14] Ava Thompson: Hey. Yep, sounds good.

[00:16] Priya Shah: Hi.

[00:18] Mark Feldman: Hey everyone.

[00:20] Dylan Brooks: Maybe Ava, you want to kick off with what you’re seeing in Console? Like, what’s working, what’s confusing.

[00:28] Ava Thompson: Yeah. So we turned on the experiment on Friday afternoon. It’s the chat route, the one we talked about — I think it’s called chat completions v2 on your side.

[00:39] Ava Thompson: We set it at 90/10, variant A is the current model, variant B is the candidate. We ran it over the weekend, which I know is like, not representative, but we wanted to make sure it didn’t blow up.

[00:52] Ava Thompson: The confusing bit is the results dashboard. It looks like the cost is down for B, which is great, but the latency chart doesn’t match what we see in our app metrics. And the guardrail badge went yellow once and then went back to green, and we couldn’t tell what exactly triggered it.

[01:12] Felix Schneider: Got it. The yellow badge is the “degraded” state, right? Not “stopped.”

[01:18] Ava Thompson: Yes. Degraded. And it said p99 regression. But when we drilled in, the p99 numbers were… like, they moved but not that much.

[01:28] Dylan Brooks: Okay. That’s super helpful. Logan, can you also just confirm what’s in the pipeline right now for those guardrail computations? Like the window and the baseline.

[01:38] Logan Wright: Yeah. So as of the current beta build, the guardrail evaluator uses a rolling 30-minute window, evaluated every five minutes. Baseline is variant A for the same window.

[01:50] Logan Wright: But — and this is one of the known gotchas — if attribution drops for a subset of requests, the denominator changes and you can get like “spiky” percent deltas. Especially at low traffic like 10%.

[02:06] Mark Feldman: That might be what’s happening because we’re mostly streaming responses and we had retries over the weekend.

[02:12] Logan Wright: Yeah. Streaming plus retries is exactly where we’ve seen attribution get lost if the request context isn’t persisted. We have a fix in flight. But for your account specifically, I want to look at your route.

[02:26] Priya Shah: When you say attribution drops, you mean it doesn’t know which variant a request was in?

[02:31] Logan Wright: Correct. The request still completes, but the experiment_id / variant_id tags don’t make it into the span and the metrics rollup. So the charts may undercount one side.

[02:45] Priya Shah: That would explain why sometimes the chart looks like it has… like missing segments.

[02:49] Felix Schneider: Yep. The UI right now doesn’t call out missing attribution strongly. We have an internal panel that shows “attribution drop rate,” but it’s not surfaced in the customer view yet.

[03:02] Ava Thompson: Right. That’s another piece — we weren’t sure if “no data” is no traffic or if it’s like dropped tags.

[03:09] Felix Schneider: Makes sense. That’s a UX gap.

[03:12] Dylan Brooks: Okay. Let’s do this: first, let’s sanity check the deltas you’re seeing. Ava, do you have the numbers handy?

[03:20] Ava Thompson: Yeah. I wrote them down because the dashboard kept changing. For Saturday roughly, variant B cost per 1K tokens was about 7 to 10% lower. Latency p95 was like 20 milliseconds higher. Error rate looked similar.

[03:38] Mark Feldman: But our app metrics show p95 roughly flat, not +20ms. That’s why we’re confused.

[03:44] Logan Wright: Are you comparing the same thing? Our p95 on the dashboard is “server-side end-to-end,” including queuing and the batching wait. Your app may be measuring client-perceived or just first-token.

[03:57] Mark Feldman: We measure time-to-first-token mostly.

[04:00] Logan Wright: Okay, yep. That’ll diverge. On our side, the tail includes full stream completion. If B reduces compute cost but increases tokens emitted, or has different streaming pacing, tail can move.

[04:16] Priya Shah: Wait, B could generate more tokens?

[04:18] Mark Feldman: It did in a few cases. We saw longer answers.

[04:22] Dylan Brooks: That’s another important point: cost charts are normalized per 1K tokens, but if output length changes, total cost per request can still increase or decrease depending.

[04:33] Ava Thompson: That’s… not clear in the dashboard today.

[04:37] Felix Schneider: Right now, we show “cost per 1K tokens” and “total tokens,” but the delta view is a bit buried. We can send you a quick guide on how to interpret it.

[04:49] Dylan Brooks: Also, we should confirm which cost metric you care about for the POC success criteria. Is it cost per token or cost per request?

[04:58] Priya Shah: For us it’s really cost per request for the chat feature. Like, what does it do to margin.

[05:04] Dylan Brooks: Got it. So we should look at both tokens/request and cost/1K tokens. We can work with you on that.

[05:12] Rafael Mendes: And from a guardrail standpoint, we can guardrail both. Like you can set a max regression for total cost per request, not just per token. For beta we’re mostly doing token economics plus latency/error.

[05:27] Ava Thompson: That’s good. Because if the model rambles, that’s not good for cost or UX.

[05:32] Mark Feldman: Plus quality.

[05:34] Dylan Brooks: Speaking of quality — have you connected any eval suite to these variants yet, or is it purely operational metrics right now?

[05:42] Mark Feldman: Operational only. We have internal evals but we didn’t wire it to your eval pipeline. Yet.

[05:48] Dylan Brooks: Okay. The GA-ish plan is that quality hooks can show up as a “quality score” stream per variant, but we can treat that as a later step. For now, let’s make sure the operational guardrails are sane.

[06:03] Priya Shah: So about the yellow “degraded.” Is it stopping traffic or just warning?

[06:09] Rafael Mendes: Currently, in your environment, it’s “soft-stop.” Meaning: we mark it degraded, we send a notification, but we do not automatically stop the experiment unless you enabled “auto stop,” which I don’t think you did.

[06:22] Ava Thompson: We did not. We saw the toggle and were scared.

[06:26] Dylan Brooks: That’s the right instinct for a POC. We’re being conservative with auto-stop because false positives are painful.

[06:33] Priya Shah: Okay. But then why did it go yellow and then green? Does it auto-resolve?

[06:39] Rafael Mendes: It resolves when the metric goes back within threshold in the next evaluation window. So if you have a spike, you can see brief degraded status.

[06:50] Felix Schneider: And the UI right now doesn’t show “evaluation window: last 30 minutes” super explicitly; it’s in the small text.

[06:58] Ava Thompson: Yeah, it’s not obvious.

[07:00] Dylan Brooks: Okay. That seems like a clear UI fix: make window and baseline explicit.

[07:07] Mark Feldman: Also, the baseline seems to jump. Like sometimes it’s comparing to “All traffic” not variant A?

[07:14] Felix Schneider: It should always compare to variant A for the delta cards. But the raw charts can be filtered by “All variants” and then the overlay looks confusing. Let me pull up the same view after and send you exact clicks.

[07:31] Priya Shah: That would be helpful.

[07:34] Dylan Brooks: Okay. Logan, can you talk about the propagation delay too? Because I remember you mentioned some lag issues.

[07:41] Logan Wright: Yeah. Config propagation: we push experiment configs through the control plane cache and then into the router policy layer. Usually it’s under a minute, but in staging we saw 5–10 minutes in some cases.

[07:54] Logan Wright: Metrics aggregation also has a delay. The “results” view is not truly real-time; it’s typically 3–7 minutes, sometimes 15 if the rollup job is backed up.

[08:05] Ava Thompson: So that might explain why we’d change the split and not see it reflected.

[08:10] Logan Wright: Exactly.

[08:12] Mark Feldman: We changed from 95/5 to 90/10, and the dashboard looked stuck for like 20 minutes.

[08:19] Logan Wright: That’s consistent with aggregation lag. We should expose “last updated” timestamp on the dashboard, if it’s not already visible.

[08:29] Felix Schneider: It’s there but again, it’s not prominent. We can make it more obvious.

[08:36] Dylan Brooks: Okay. Let’s talk guardrails tuning. Right now what thresholds are you using?

[08:42] Ava Thompson: We just left the defaults. I think it was p99 latency 25% regression, error rate 1% absolute, and cost per token 15% increase? Something like that.

[08:55] Rafael Mendes: That’s close. Defaults are: p99 latency regression threshold 20% over baseline; error rate absolute +0.5% (depends on route type); and cost per 1K tokens +10%.

[09:08] Ava Thompson: Okay.

[09:10] Priya Shah: Are those defaults tuned for chat? Because our baseline error rate is like, really low.

[09:17] Rafael Mendes: The defaults are generic. For your baseline, we can tighten error threshold because a small absolute increase might be meaningful. But we also don’t want noise to spam you at 10% traffic.

[09:29] Mark Feldman: Yeah, at 10% the tails are noisy.

[09:33] Logan Wright: Exactly. At low traffic, p99 is not stable. If you want guardrails to be meaningful at 10%, I’d recommend using p95 as the alerting guardrail and reserve p99 for higher splits.

[09:48] Ava Thompson: That would match what we’re seeing. The p99 card is like a yo-yo.

[09:53] Rafael Mendes: We can do that. We can set p95 regression threshold now, and then once you ramp to 20% or 50%, enable the p99 guardrail.

[10:04] Priya Shah: Does the UI allow that? Like selecting which percentile?

[10:08] Felix Schneider: In the wizard, yes, there’s a dropdown for percentile. But it’s a bit hidden under “advanced.”

[10:15] Ava Thompson: Okay.

[10:16] Dylan Brooks: Another thing: we should align on what “stop” means. If you manually stop, is it immediate?

[10:22] Rafael Mendes: “Stop” is immediate in terms of routing decisions: new requests stop being assigned to variants and go back to baseline policy. There can be in-flight streams, obviously.

[10:32] Mark Feldman: That’s fine.

[10:34] Dylan Brooks: Okay. Now, can we dig into the latency mismatch? Mark, you mentioned your app’s p95 is flat.

[10:42] Mark Feldman: Yeah. We instrument TTFT and total time. TTFT is flat, total time is slightly higher for B, but not by 20ms. More like 5ms.

[10:55] Logan Wright: We should verify we’re comparing the same region and the same route normalization. If your route id is templated on our side, it should be stable. But if you’re hitting multiple endpoints, the dashboard might be aggregating.

[11:10] Ava Thompson: We only hit one endpoint, but we have eu-west and us-east.

[11:14] Logan Wright: Aha. The dashboard default view is “All regions.” If eu-west is noisier and has lower traffic, it can skew p99 and p95.

[11:25] Ava Thompson: Oh. That might be it.

[11:27] Felix Schneider: We can also add a callout: “You are viewing aggregated across regions” because that is not obvious.

[11:34] Priya Shah: Yeah, it’s super easy to miss.

[11:38] Dylan Brooks: Okay. So immediate next step: Logan, can you look at their attribution and split by region and streaming.

[11:46] Logan Wright: Yep.

[11:47] Dylan Brooks: Felix, can you send them a “how to read this dashboard” note? Like two screenshots with arrows.

[11:53] Felix Schneider: Yeah, I can do that.

[11:56] Ava Thompson: Thank you.

[11:58] Dylan Brooks: Now, about cost: You said 7–10% down per 1K tokens for B. That’s in line with what we expected if B is using the quantized profile, right?

[12:09] Mark Feldman: Yes. But we also saw tokens/request go up like 3%. So net savings is smaller.

[12:16] Dylan Brooks: Understood. For the POC we probably want a “cost per request” delta as the top-line.

[12:23] Priya Shah: Agreed.

[12:25] Felix Schneider: We don’t currently show “cost per request” as the primary card; it’s on the drill-down. But we can at least point you there.

[12:33] Ava Thompson: Okay.

[12:35] Dylan Brooks: Any other dashboard confusion?

[12:38] Ava Thompson: The variant headers. We saw headers like x-redwood-experiment and x-redwood-variant, but sometimes they’re missing, especially on streaming.

[12:48] Logan Wright: That’s related to attribution context too. In general they should be present, but if your client library strips them or if it’s a retry path, we’ve seen them disappear.

[12:58] Mark Feldman: We’re using your Python SDK, but we also have an API gateway that might be dropping headers.

[13:05] Dylan Brooks: Okay. If those headers are important for your debugging, we should make sure you’re allowing them through.

[13:12] Priya Shah: That’s on us, we can check.

[13:14] Rafael Mendes: And also, we can tune the system so it logs assignment decisions server-side. There’s an internal debug endpoint, but we don’t expose that to customers. For POC, Logan can provide a report.

[13:28] Ava Thompson: Great.

[13:30] Dylan Brooks: Let’s talk ramp plan. You’re at 90/10 now. Are you comfortable going to 80/20?

[13:38] Ava Thompson: If we can get confidence that the degraded badge is not like, real regression, yes. We just don’t want to surprise ourselves.

[13:49] Mark Feldman: And we want to set guardrails that aren’t noisy.

[13:52] Rafael Mendes: So here’s what I propose: for this week, use p95 regression guardrail, not p99; use a longer evaluation window, like 60 minutes, to reduce noise; and keep soft-stop.

[14:06] Priya Shah: Does longer window make it slower to catch issues?

[14:09] Rafael Mendes: Yes. It’s a tradeoff. But at 20% traffic, you’ll still catch meaningful issues. Another approach is two-tier: short window for severe regressions only.

[14:20] Dylan Brooks: For POC, I’d optimize for not spamming you with false alarms.

[14:24] Ava Thompson: Same.

[14:26] Mark Feldman: What about error rate? We had like two 503s but I think it was our gateway.

[14:32] Logan Wright: If you’re seeing 503, check if they’re upstream timeouts. On our side we track error codes. We can break it down by status class per variant.

[14:43] Ava Thompson: That would be helpful.

[14:45] Dylan Brooks: Okay. Another question: stickiness key. What are you using?

[14:51] Ava Thompson: Right now we pass customer_id, not end_user_id.

[14:56] Dylan Brooks: That means each tenant is sticky to a variant. That’s okay for fairness across tenants, but it can cause issues if some tenants have different traffic patterns.

[15:07] Mark Feldman: We were thinking end_user_id but we don’t always have it.

[15:12] Logan Wright: If end_user_id is missing sometimes, that can cause skew because we fall back to a less stable key. That’s a known problem.

[15:21] Ava Thompson: Like what does it fall back to?

[15:23] Logan Wright: Potentially request_id or IP-ish signals depending on integration, which are low entropy / not stable across retries.

[15:31] Ava Thompson: Okay, that sounds bad.

[15:33] Rafael Mendes: For POC, customer_id is okay if you have enough tenants. How many tenants are in this traffic?

[15:41] Ava Thompson: About 120 active. But top ten are 60% of traffic.

[15:46] Rafael Mendes: That’s borderline. You could get one big tenant mostly in variant B and then your results are biased.

[15:54] Mark Feldman: Exactly.

[15:56] Dylan Brooks: Could you do a composite key? Like customer_id + end_user_id when present, else customer_id + session?

[16:04] Ava Thompson: Maybe. But session isn’t stable across devices.

[16:08] Logan Wright: Another approach is: always send a stable “experiment sticky key” generated by you. If you can create a hashed user identifier that’s always present, that’s best.

[16:20] Mark Feldman: We can do that. It might take a few days.

[16:23] Priya Shah: That’s fine.

[16:25] Dylan Brooks: Cool. That’ll make results much more trustworthy.

[16:29] Felix Schneider: And it also helps with debugging because the assignment is deterministic. Same key, same bucket, same variant.

[16:36] Ava Thompson: Got it.

[16:38] Dylan Brooks: Okay, last topic: what do you need from us to feel comfortable presenting early results internally?

[16:46] Priya Shah: Honestly we need a narrative. Like: what metrics matter, why the dashboard may not match our app charts, and what “good” looks like. Right now it’s too easy for someone to screenshot the yellow badge and panic.

[16:59] Dylan Brooks: Understood. We can send a short write-up: “How to interpret results in beta,” plus known limitations.

[17:06] Felix Schneider: I can draft that. It’s basically: time window, baseline, aggregation delay, and region filters.

[17:12] Priya Shah: Great.

[17:14] Mark Feldman: Also if you can confirm whether our attribution is healthy. That’s the biggest trust issue.

[17:20] Logan Wright: Yep. I’ll pull it. If we see high attribution drop rate, we’ll tell you and we can either fix integration or we can hold off on ramp.

[17:30] Ava Thompson: Perfect.

[17:32] Dylan Brooks: Alright. Let’s do a quick recap and next steps.

[17:36] Dylan Brooks: Logan will run the attribution checks and split by region and streaming; Rafael will propose tuned guardrails (p95 vs p99, 60-min window); Felix will send dashboard reading guide and a short narrative note; and you all will confirm the stickiness key plan and header pass-through.

[17:55] Ava Thompson: Yep.

[17:56] Priya Shah: Sounds good.

[17:58] Mark Feldman: Yep.

[18:00] Dylan Brooks: Cool. Before we end, any other questions?

[18:04] Ava Thompson: Just one. If we ramp to 20%, how long before we should expect stable p95 and cost per request deltas?

[18:12] Logan Wright: If you run a full weekday, you’ll get much more stable. Weekend patterns can distort. I’d say at least 24 hours with typical traffic.

[18:22] Rafael Mendes: And for guardrails, at 20% you’ll reduce the noise a lot.

[18:27] Dylan Brooks: Great. We’ll schedule next week same time.

[18:31] Priya Shah: Works for us.

[18:33] Dylan Brooks: Thanks all.

---

Note: Transcript is auto-generated and may contain errors (proper nouns, product names, acronyms, and speaker attribution).

title: Weekly POC check-in — experiment results + guardrails (Northbridge)
preview: Northbridge walked through their first 48 hours of route-level experiment traffic. They are seeing expected cost reductions in variant B but are confused by several Console dashboard behaviors (time window alignment, baseline comparison, and what triggers a guardrail badge). Redwood clarified current semantics (soft-stop defaults, guardrail evaluation windows, known propagation lag and attribution drop issues) and agreed on changes: clearer UI copy/tooltips for deltas, making the evaluation window explicit, and tightening guidance around stickiness keys and streaming retry attribution. Next steps include Redwood validating attribution integrity for their route, suggesting tuned thresholds for p99 latency and error rate, and a short enablement note on how to interpret variant headers and results freshness.
text: Northbridge walked through their first 48 hours of route-level experiment traffic. They are seeing expected cost reductions in variant B but are confused by several Console dashboard behaviors (time window alignment, baseline comparison, and what triggers a guardrail badge). Redwood clarified current semantics (soft-stop defaults, guardrail evaluation windows, known propagation lag and attribution drop issues) and agreed on changes: clearer UI copy/tooltips for deltas, making the evaluation window explicit, and tightening guidance around stickiness keys and streaming retry attribution. Next steps include Redwood validating attribution integrity for their route, suggesting tuned thresholds for p99 latency and error rate, and a short enablement note on how to interpret variant headers and results freshness.

source.title: POC scoping - route experiments / A-B testing (Northbridge)
source.summary: Northbridge is scoping a POC to run route-level A/B tests between model variants (cost vs latency vs output quality) and wants first-class measurement in Redwood Console: p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate, throughput, and cost per request / cost per 1k tokens broken out by variant. They need deterministic assignment (stickiness) keyed off their end-user or account id, plus the ability to ramp traffic safely with guardrails (soft-stop initially ok, but they want an option for auto-stop for severe p99 regressions). They also raised enterprise requirements: RBAC on who can start/stop experiments, audit logging of config changes, and clarity on how experiment attribution shows up in logs/headers for debugging. Timeline: start POC in early March, run for ~2 weeks, decision by late March for an April/May rollout on a subset of routes.
topics: POC goals and success criteria (cost, latency, quality)
Traffic splits, ramp schedule, and guardrails
Metrics attribution in Console + export requirements
questions: Can we pin model versions per variant and keep the same prompt/params across both?
How do you handle missing stickiness keys (what happens to assignment)?
Can we see cost broken out by prompt vs completion tokens per variant?
What’s the expected freshness/lag of experiment results in Console?
Is there a way to export experiment audit events for compliance review?
next_steps: Redwood to share POC checklist + recommended ramp plan (10% -> 25% -> 50%) with suggested guardrail defaults.
Northbridge to confirm preferred stickiness key (end_user_id vs account_id) and whether they can reliably send it on all routes.
Redwood to provide a short write-up on cost attribution definitions (streaming, retries, cached tokens) and how it appears in Console.
Schedule a follow-up technical session to walk through the experiment creation workflow in Console (beta) and debug headers/log fields.
Northbridge security to send their audit log / change management evidence requirements (fields, retention, export format).
action_items: Marissa Cole - Email POC success criteria template + beta enablement requirements to Northbridge - Due 2025-02-20
Dylan Brooks - Share example experiment config (2 variants, 90/10 ramp) and explain precedence with fallback policies - Due 2025-02-21
Logan Wright - Send metric definitions for latency (p50/p95/p99), cost attribution per variant, and known limitations (cardinality, sampling) - Due 2025-02-21
Sarah Kim - Confirm which routes/endpoints are in scope for the POC and expected baseline QPS - Due 2025-02-24
Renee Zhao - Provide compliance/audit logging requirements checklist (who/what/when fields) - Due 2025-02-24
source.transcript:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Title: POC scoping - route experiments / A-B testing (Northbridge)
Date: 2025-02-18
Time: 10:00 AM - 10:57 AM PT
Duration: 57 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
- Redwood Inference: Marissa Cole, Dylan Brooks, Logan Wright
- Northbridge: Sarah Kim, David Patel, Renee Zhao

---

[00:00] Marissa Cole: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for joining. I know we’ve got, uh, Sarah, David, Renee on from Northbridge. I’m Marissa, I lead Console and Rollouts product on the Redwood side. Dylan’s our engineering lead for Console tooling, and Logan’s leading telemetry and analytics.

[00:18] Sarah Kim: Great. Thanks, Marissa. Yeah, we pulled in David because he’s hands-on with our model eval pipeline and the app integration. Renee’s here mostly for… the usual, like audit logs and change management.

[00:32] Marissa Cole: Perfect. So I think the purpose today is POC scoping. You mentioned you’re looking for A/B testing, route-level, to compare model variants. Maybe start with the problem statement in your words and what success looks like.

[00:47] Sarah Kim: Sure. We have, like, three production-ish routes that are LLM-backed. It’s basically a chat endpoint, an extraction endpoint, and then a rerank-ish endpoint. We’re currently on a mix of providers and we’re trying to consolidate. We want to be able to test model A versus model B—could be two different sizes or quantizations—without rebuilding our own experimentation harness.

[01:12] Sarah Kim: And the big thing: we don’t just need “conversion,” we need latency and cost. Like p95, p99. And cost per request, or cost per 1,000 tokens, something. And we want it in the same place we operate rollouts.

[01:30] Marissa Cole: Got it. So Console as the operational hub, plus measurement.

[01:34] David Patel: Yeah and quality. Like, we have internal eval sets. For this POC we can do a simplified version. But, like, if model B is cheaper but it’s hallucinating more, we need to see that.

[01:46] Logan Wright: Yep. That maps to how we’re thinking about it. For the initial scoping, do you want quality to be automated in the Console or is it acceptable to run your eval and then we just compare the telemetry metrics in Console?

[02:02] David Patel: For the POC, it’s okay to do it out-of-band. But we’d like at least a hook so results can be tagged per variant. We can run the eval harness nightly and then… if you can show a “quality score” tile, great. If not, we can live without it for two weeks.

[02:22] Marissa Cole: Makes sense. For the POC, the core is route-level experiments with deterministic assignment and metrics by variant.

[02:30] Dylan Brooks: Sarah, can you clarify what you mean by “route” in your system? Is it literally the HTTP path, like /v1/chat/completions, or is it a higher-level thing like “feature: support agent”?

[02:42] Sarah Kim: It’s basically an API path. We have our own gateway. We can pass headers downstream. But for Redwood, we’re probably calling your chat completions endpoint with a “route id” we set, if that’s a thing.

[02:56] Dylan Brooks: Yep, we can talk about the naming. We typically normalize to a “route_id” dimension to avoid high cardinality on raw paths.

[03:06] Renee Zhao: Sorry, can I just jump in with a question? When you say “pass headers,” do you need us to pass PII? Because we have a policy that user identifiers must be hashed or pseudonymous. We can do that, but it has to be stable.

[03:21] Marissa Cole: Good question. The stickiness key can be a stable pseudonymous identifier. We don’t need email or anything. A lot of customers use account_id or user_id hashed.

[03:31] Logan Wright: And we can also accept a customer-provided “sticky key” header. If that’s missing, we have fallbacks, but missing keys can cause skew, so we’ll want to make it reliable.

[03:44] David Patel: Yeah, we can send “x-nb-user” or “x-nb-account” hashed. The only issue is some requests are service-to-service, not tied to a user.

[03:55] Dylan Brooks: In those cases, you can pick a stable key per job or per tenant. If it’s truly random per request, the assignment won’t be sticky, but distribution will be okay. The bigger problem is low-entropy keys like “unknown”.

[04:11] Sarah Kim: Right, we did that once and it… yeah it was bad. Everything went to one bucket.

[04:16] Logan Wright: Exactly. The hash needs entropy.

[04:20] Marissa Cole: Okay, so if we summarize goals. You want: (1) A/B between model variants on a route, (2) deterministic assignment, (3) traffic ramp controls, (4) metrics in Console for latency, cost, errors, throughput, and optionally quality.

[04:37] Sarah Kim: Yep. And quick stop. Like, if p99 goes to the moon, we need to roll back.

[04:44] Marissa Cole: Understood. Let’s dig into success criteria for the POC. What would make you say “yes, this works, we’ll commit to Redwood”?

[04:53] Sarah Kim: For us, success looks like: we can run a two-variant experiment on our chat route. Variant A is our baseline model. Variant B is a cheaper one. We can ramp from 10% to 50% within like a week, with guardrails.

[05:12] Sarah Kim: And then in Console we can see a dashboard that says p95, p99, error rate, and cost per 1k tokens, per variant, and it updates reasonably quickly. Doesn’t have to be realtime, but not like 24 hours.

[05:27] Logan Wright: What’s “reasonably quickly” for you? 5 minutes? 30 minutes? An hour?

[05:33] Sarah Kim: Hourly is fine. Maybe 15 minutes would be better. But hourly is okay.

[05:40] Logan Wright: Okay. We’ll be transparent: for the POC we can commit to, like, 15-60 minutes depending on aggregation windows. We’ve seen customers operate experiments with 30-minute windows.

[05:51] David Patel: And cost attribution—how are you defining cost? Like is it provider cost or token-based billing?

[05:58] Marissa Cole: For Redwood it’s token-based. In Console we break out prompt tokens and completion tokens. Logan, do you want to talk about what’s available per variant?

[06:08] Logan Wright: Yeah. The event attribution includes experiment_id and variant_id, and then we can aggregate tokens and latency by those. We can show cost as “estimated billable units” times rate. For Dedicated capacity it can be more nuanced, but for Hosted API it’s pretty straightforward.

[06:26] David Patel: We’re likely starting hosted for the POC.

[06:28] Marissa Cole: Great.

[06:30] Renee Zhao: For me, success is also: can we see who changed an experiment config and when. Like, start/stop, and traffic split changes. That’s audit.

[06:41] Marissa Cole: Yep. Audit logging is built into the experiment lifecycle. We can export it. We can talk about the exact fields.

[06:50] Renee Zhao: Okay.

[06:52] Dylan Brooks: Can I ask about your baseline volumes? Like QPS, tokens per request, concurrency. It matters for how quickly metrics stabilize and also for cost.

[07:03] Sarah Kim: Chat route is around 40 QPS peak, maybe 10 QPS average. Extraction is lower. Tokens per request… depends. Average maybe 2k in and 500 out? Something like that.

[07:16] David Patel: For chat, yeah, and we stream sometimes.

[07:20] Logan Wright: Streaming is fine. We do need to be careful about attribution on retries and streaming restarts, but for POC we can validate the drop rate.

[07:30] David Patel: Drop rate meaning…?

[07:33] Logan Wright: Like, percentage of requests where the experiment tags don’t make it to metrics because of some async path. We’ve been tightening it. We’ll watch it.

[07:44] Marissa Cole: On the experiment config itself: do you want just two variants or A/B/n?

[07:49] Sarah Kim: Two for now.

[07:51] Marissa Cole: Any needs for eligibility rules? Like only for a subset of users, or only for certain regions?

[07:58] Sarah Kim: Potentially region. We’re mostly US and EU. For POC we can do US only.

[08:05] Dylan Brooks: Great. That reduces cross-region determinism concerns.

[08:09] David Patel: But longer-term we’d want EU too. We’ve had weirdness where an “experiment” run in EU and US yields different splits.

[08:18] Dylan Brooks: We can make it consistent as long as the same key and same hashing function are used. There are edge cases if the config propagates at different times between regions, but we measure propagation lag.

[08:31] Sarah Kim: That’s a concern. Like if we change split from 10 to 50, we want it to take effect everywhere at roughly the same time.

[08:38] Marissa Cole: Totally. For POC we’ll set expectations on config propagation. We have staleness bounds in the control plane.

[08:47] Logan Wright: And we can show a “config version” in the debug output so you can confirm what router decision was made.

[08:56] David Patel: Debug output—like an endpoint? Or a header?

[09:00] Dylan Brooks: Both. We can return response headers like x-redwood-experiment-id and x-redwood-variant-id, and there’s an internal “explain assignment” endpoint we can enable for support scenarios.

[09:13] Renee Zhao: Internal endpoint sounds like something we’d need to gate behind RBAC.

[09:17] Dylan Brooks: Yes, it’s restricted. For customers, we typically rely on headers and Console.

[09:23] Marissa Cole: On guardrails: you mentioned p99. Do you have specific thresholds?

[09:28] Sarah Kim: Our current p99 is like 2.8 seconds for chat, which is not great but it’s acceptable for now. If it goes above 4 seconds, we want to stop. For p95 we try to keep it under 1.5.

[09:45] David Patel: And error rate. If variant starts throwing 5xx, we need to stop quickly.

[09:51] Marissa Cole: Ok. So guardrails: p99 latency > 4s, error rate maybe > 1%? what’s your tolerance.

[09:58] Sarah Kim: If it’s above 0.5% sustained, we’ll stop.

[10:03] Logan Wright: We can do a sliding window. The question is whether you want auto-stop (hard stop) or just alerts and then someone clicks stop.

[10:12] Sarah Kim: For POC, alert is okay, but we’d like the option to auto-stop for the severe cases. Like if p99 is 2x baseline.

[10:24] Marissa Cole: That’s aligned with our current approach: soft-stop by default with notifications; optional hard-stop behind a kill switch for severe regressions.

[10:35] Renee Zhao: When you say “kill switch,” you mean your operator can disable auto-stop globally?

[10:40] Marissa Cole: Yes, and we also scope it per account. We’ve had customers concerned about false positives.

[10:48] Renee Zhao: Okay. As long as it’s auditable.

[10:52] Dylan Brooks: Another guardrail we’ve seen is “variant gets zero traffic” due to misconfig or missing key. We alert on that.

[11:00] Sarah Kim: That would be helpful.

[11:02] Marissa Cole: Let’s talk about the actual models. For the POC, which baseline are you on? And what’s the candidate cheaper model.

[11:11] David Patel: Baseline is basically a 70B-ish model. Not naming names, but comparable to, you know, Llama 70B class. Candidate is a 13B or 34B with better quantization. We’re evaluating.

[11:26] Dylan Brooks: In Redwood terms, that can be either different model_id’s or same model with different “profile” (quantization / kernel). In an experiment variant, you can point to a different model or different serving profile.

[11:40] Sarah Kim: We’d like it to be literally two model IDs so it’s clear.

[11:45] Marissa Cole: That’s fine.

[11:47] David Patel: Also, we might want variant B to have slightly different parameters. Like temperature or max tokens.

[11:55] Marissa Cole: We can support variant-specific overrides, but we recommend holding params constant for cleaner interpretation. For POC, maybe keep it consistent.

[12:07] David Patel: Fair.

[12:09] Logan Wright: On cost, do you want to see “cost per request” or “cost per successful request”? because retries and errors can skew it.

[12:18] Sarah Kim: Cost per request is fine, but we need to understand the definition. If a retry happens, does it count twice?

[12:26] Logan Wright: Usually yes, because tokens are consumed. We can also show cost per successful request separately if we aggregate with status code dimensions.

[12:35] Sarah Kim: That’d be helpful.

[12:37] Renee Zhao: Also, do you retain prompts? Because we have data retention constraints.

[12:42] Marissa Cole: By default, we don’t store full prompts. We keep metadata and metrics. For Private deployments there are options, but for hosted we keep minimal logs. We can follow up with the standard security doc.

[12:55] Renee Zhao: Please do.

[12:57] Dylan Brooks: Another scoping question: Are you expecting to do this through UI only, or API-driven? Like does your platform team want to automate experiment creation.

[13:07] Sarah Kim: For POC, UI is fine. Longer term, API is better. But if the UI is gated behind feature flag, we can work with you.

[13:16] Marissa Cole: UI is in beta. We can enable it for your account.

[13:20] Sarah Kim: Okay.

[13:22] Marissa Cole: One thing we should align on: route experiments interact with rollouts and fallback policies. Are you using canary rollouts today?

[13:31] Sarah Kim: Not in Redwood, because we’re not on Redwood yet. In our current infra we do canary. We’d like to eventually use your rollouts.

[13:40] Marissa Cole: Okay. For POC we can keep it simpler: one route, two variants, no other rollouts.

[13:47] Dylan Brooks: Just note: if you have a fallback policy, the experiment selection happens before fallback. So if the variant model is unavailable, fallback can kick in, but the attribution stays consistent.

[14:01] David Patel: That’s important because we don’t want fallback to “erase” the assignment.

[14:06] Dylan Brooks: Exactly.

[14:08] Logan Wright: And the metrics will show the variant assignment even if fallback served a different underlying model, but we can also tag “served_model_id” so you can see that. It helps debug.

[14:20] Sarah Kim: Nice.

[14:22] Marissa Cole: Alright, can we talk timeline. When do you want to start the POC and when do you want to decide.

[14:29] Sarah Kim: We’re trying to start early March. Ideally week of March 3rd or 10th. Run for two weeks. Make a decision by March 28th.

[14:41] David Patel: And if it’s a yes, we’d roll out to maybe 20% of production traffic in April, then more in May.

[14:50] Marissa Cole: Got it. That lines up with our GA timeframe for the experiments feature, but for you we can do beta.

[14:57] Renee Zhao: For timeline, we’ll need security review materials soon. Like this week.

[15:02] Marissa Cole: We can send SOC2 summary, data retention, encryption, RBAC, and audit logging docs.

[15:10] Renee Zhao: Okay.

[15:12] Logan Wright: On the measurement side, one call-out: if you’re looking for statistically significant deltas, we can show basic deltas but we’re not doing a full significance engine in the beta.

[15:22] Sarah Kim: That’s okay. We’re fine interpreting. We just need accurate metrics.

[15:27] David Patel: Exactly. We have our own internal analysis.

[15:31] Marissa Cole: Good. For POC, what’s the key question you’re trying to answer. Is it “cheaper model is acceptable quality at same latency” or “we can reduce cost while staying under latency SLO.”

[15:42] Sarah Kim: It’s primarily cost reduction without violating p99 and without tanking our eval score too much.

[15:49] David Patel: And we’d like to see if a mid-size model can actually be faster. Sometimes it’s slower because it doesn’t batch well.

[15:58] Dylan Brooks: Yeah, batching and KV cache behavior can make it weird. We can advise on configs.

[16:04] Sarah Kim: Great.

[16:06] Dylan Brooks: Can I ask about request identity. You said you can pass hashed user or account. Which do you prefer for stickiness?

[16:14] Sarah Kim: Probably account_id. User_id changes too much. Account is stable.

[16:19] David Patel: But for chat, user-level might be better to avoid weird cross-user sharing of variant assignment. But account-level is okay.

[16:28] Logan Wright: We usually recommend the “entity” that you want consistent experience for. If users within an account should see same model, use account_id. If not, use user_id.

[16:39] Sarah Kim: For POC, account_id is easiest.

[16:42] Marissa Cole: Okay.

[16:44] Renee Zhao: And you don’t need us to pass IP addresses or anything, right.

[16:48] Marissa Cole: No.

[16:50] Dylan Brooks: In our precedence, we look for explicit sticky key header first, then fall back to some request context. But we’ll tell you exactly what header name.

[17:00] Sarah Kim: Great.

[17:02] Marissa Cole: Let’s talk about what you expect in Console. Sarah, you want per-variant charts. Do you want time series and also aggregate summary?

[17:10] Sarah Kim: Both. Like, show p95 over time, and then summary “variant B is +12% p95, -20% cost.”

[17:20] Logan Wright: That’s doable. We compute deltas versus baseline variant.

[17:24] David Patel: And token breakdown, prompt vs completion.

[17:28] Logan Wright: Yep.

[17:30] Sarah Kim: Also, if there’s an “attribution drop rate,” I’d like to see it. Because we don’t want silent errors in metrics.

[17:38] Logan Wright: Love that you asked. Yes. We have a guardrail dashboard for that.

[17:44] Dylan Brooks: One UI note: We show guardrail status. Like green/yellow/red. If p99 crosses threshold, it’s red.

[17:52] Sarah Kim: Good.

[17:54] Marissa Cole: For POC, do you need export? Like CSV or API for metrics.

[18:01] Sarah Kim: Ideally API. But if it’s a screenshot, we can survive. For audit events, Renee will want export.

[18:09] Renee Zhao: Yes. Even if it’s not formal SIEM integration, we need something like JSON lines export.

[18:16] Marissa Cole: We can provide an audit export format. We’ll share examples.

[18:21] Renee Zhao: Okay.

[18:23] David Patel: Another question: If we stop the experiment, does traffic immediately go to baseline, or does it drain.

[18:31] Dylan Brooks: Good question. Stop is effectively immediate at routing. In-flight requests continue. We can also have a “draining” state where config is disabled but we still display results.

[18:44] Marissa Cole: From a UI standpoint, we’ll show “Stopping” briefly if propagation is in progress, then “Stopped.”

[18:52] Sarah Kim: Ok.

[18:54] Logan Wright: And metrics continue to populate for the period after stop because aggregation windows lag.

[19:00] Sarah Kim: Makes sense.

[19:02] Marissa Cole: We should also cover RBAC. Who at Northbridge would be allowed to create/start/stop experiments.

[19:10] Sarah Kim: Platform team only. Not application engineers.

[19:14] Renee Zhao: And there should be a break-glass admin role. But we want that auditable.

[19:19] Marissa Cole: Yes, we have admin roles and we log actor, action, old/new values.

[19:26] Renee Zhao: Good.

[19:28] Dylan Brooks: For guardrail auto-stop, is your preference: Redwood automatically stops and sends notification to Slack/email, or do you want a webhook.

[19:38] Sarah Kim: Slack would be fine. Webhook later.

[19:42] Marissa Cole: Okay.

[19:44] David Patel: Can you talk about how you compute p99. Because we’ve been burned where p99 is computed on too small samples.

[19:53] Logan Wright: Yeah. We compute quantiles on a rolling window. If sample size is small, we can show “insufficient data” and avoid triggering hard stops on low n. For POC, we can configure minimum request counts.

[20:08] David Patel: Great.

[20:10] Marissa Cole: Any constraints about running experiments simultaneously across routes.

[20:16] Sarah Kim: For POC, we only run one at a time.

[20:19] Dylan Brooks: Great. We do have validation to avoid overlapping experiments on the same route. It simplifies precedence.

[20:27] Sarah Kim: Good.

[20:29] Marissa Cole: Quick detour: you mentioned you’re evaluating other providers. What are you currently using for A/B testing.

[20:37] Sarah Kim: We built a DIY thing in our gateway, but it doesn’t have consistent telemetry. We looked at Langfuse for tracing, but it’s not really routing experiments. Bedrock has some stuff but it’s not ergonomic.

[20:50] David Patel: OpenAI doesn’t help here either. We’d still have to implement the routing.

[20:56] Marissa Cole: Okay.

[20:58] Logan Wright: For cost, since you mentioned providers: do you need us to compare “apples to apples” across vendors. Like Redwood cost vs Bedrock cost.

[21:08] Sarah Kim: Not inside Console. We’ll do that. We just need your cost and tokens.

[21:14] Logan Wright: Cool.

[21:16] Marissa Cole: Let’s define the POC experiment. I’ll propose: Experiment “nb-chat-model-compare” on route “chat”. Variants: A = baseline model_id, B = candidate model_id. Allocation starts 90/10, then 75/25, then 50/50. Guardrails: error rate > 0.5% (10-min window), p99 > 4s (30-min window) triggers soft-stop alert; optionally hard-stop if p99 > 2x baseline and sample size > N.

[21:47] Sarah Kim: That sounds right.

[21:49] David Patel: For quality, we’ll run an eval set at night, and we’d like to tag the results per variant. How do we get the variant assignment in the logs.

[22:00] Dylan Brooks: We can add headers to responses and also include it in request logs. If you call Redwood directly, you’ll see “x-redwood-experiment-id” and “x-redwood-experiment-variant.” In SDKs, we can also surface it.

[22:16] David Patel: Perfect.

[22:18] Logan Wright: If you run evals by replaying prompts, you can also pin the variant by providing an override header to force variant A or B. But we should be careful with that, as it can bias metrics.

[22:31] Sarah Kim: For evals we’ll probably run separately anyway. But that’s helpful.

[22:36] Marissa Cole: Yeah, for POC, keep production assignment as-is, eval runs can force.

[22:43] Renee Zhao: For forcing variants, is that restricted? Because someone could force the expensive variant and run up cost.

[22:51] Marissa Cole: It would be restricted by API key permissions. We can gate “force variant” behind an admin role.

[22:59] Renee Zhao: Okay.

[23:01] Dylan Brooks: Also worth mentioning: we’ll cap number of variants and enforce allocation sums to 100%. Console validation catches common issues.

[23:10] Sarah Kim: Great.

[23:12] Marissa Cole: Let’s talk dependencies. For us to enable this POC, we need (1) your account set up, (2) feature flag enablement for route experiments UI, (3) agreement on stickiness key header, (4) choose models.

[23:28] Sarah Kim: We can do account and header quickly. Models we’re still deciding.

[23:33] David Patel: We can pick two by end of next week.

[23:36] Marissa Cole: Okay.

[23:38] Logan Wright: One more thing: do you need segmentation by tenant. Like a multi-tenant experiment where you only include certain accounts.

[23:46] Sarah Kim: Maybe later. For POC, no.

[23:50] Marissa Cole: Great.

[23:52] Dylan Brooks: On integration: are you calling Redwood from backend services or from client.

[23:57] Sarah Kim: Backend services.

[23:59] Dylan Brooks: Great. That makes it easier to pass stickiness headers reliably.

[24:04] David Patel: We use TypeScript services.

[24:07] Dylan Brooks: Perfect, our TS SDK supports it.

[24:11] Marissa Cole: Okay. Renee, any other security concerns.

[24:15] Renee Zhao: Just standard: SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logs, data retention, encryption at rest and in transit. And if this is beta, I need to know if there are any limitations or “best effort” items.

[24:31] Marissa Cole: For beta: the main limitation is we’re not offering full automated statistical analysis, and some features like custom metrics ingestion are out of scope. But RBAC and audit logging are core.

[24:44] Renee Zhao: Okay. And does experiment configuration include any secrets.

[24:49] Dylan Brooks: No, it’s just routing config.

[24:52] Renee Zhao: Okay.

[24:54] Sarah Kim: Can you show the UI? Or is that for another call.

[24:59] Marissa Cole: We didn’t plan a screen share today, but we can schedule a quick 30-minute follow-up with a walkthrough.

[25:06] Sarah Kim: Let’s do that.

[25:08] Marissa Cole: Great.

[25:10] Logan Wright: One more measurement question: do you need cost per 1k tokens, or cost per “output token,” or both.

[25:18] Sarah Kim: Cost per 1k total tokens is fine, but if we can split prompt vs completion, even better.

[25:26] Logan Wright: Yep.

[25:28] David Patel: Also throughput, like tokens per second. Some models are slower in decode.

[25:34] Logan Wright: We have tokens/sec and time-to-first-token (TTFT) for streaming.

[25:40] David Patel: Great.

[25:42] Marissa Cole: Great. Let’s do a quick check on ownership. Sarah, who’s the POC owner.

[25:48] Sarah Kim: Me.

[25:49] Marissa Cole: Great.

[25:51] Marissa Cole: We’ll send a POC checklist and a draft ramp plan. Also I’ll send the security packet to Renee.

[26:01] Renee Zhao: Thank you.

[26:03] Dylan Brooks: And I’ll send example config, plus how we handle fallback and how to interpret headers.

[26:09] Sarah Kim: Great.

[26:11] Logan Wright: I’ll send metric definitions. Also, note: we’ll want to confirm your baseline metrics first for the chat route. We can run baseline for a day, then start ramp.

[26:23] Sarah Kim: That’s fine.

[26:25] David Patel: Baseline for a day is okay.

[26:27] Marissa Cole: Nice.

[26:29] (crosstalk)

[26:30] Sarah Kim: Sorry, one more question. If we change traffic split from 10% to 25%, do users move buckets.

[26:38] Dylan Brooks: Great question. With deterministic bucketing, the boundary changes, so some users will move. But it’s consistent and reproducible. You can think of it as “hash mod 10k,” and allocation ranges map to variants.

[26:52] David Patel: So if we ramp up, some users previously in A might move to B.

[26:57] Dylan Brooks: Yes. If you want strict holdout, we can do that with a fixed cohort, but that’s more complex. For most routing experiments, ramp implies some churn.

[27:07] Sarah Kim: Okay, that’s acceptable.

[27:10] Logan Wright: And we can expose “assignment percentile” in debug mode if needed, but usually not necessary.

[27:17] Sarah Kim: Okay.

[27:19] Renee Zhao: Another quick question. For audit logs, do you include the diff. Like old split and new split.

[27:25] Marissa Cole: Yes, we include a change summary: old allocation, new allocation, who did it, timestamp, and the route(s) impacted.

[27:36] Renee Zhao: Great.

[27:38] Marissa Cole: Cool. We’re at the half hour mark. I think we’ve got what we need. But maybe we can spend the next 10 minutes on open questions and risks.

[27:48] Sarah Kim: The biggest risk is data quality. We’ve had dashboards lie to us.

[27:53] Logan Wright: Yep. We’ll explicitly monitor attribution drop rate and config propagation lag. For POC, we can also run a synthetic test where we send known sticky keys and verify split distribution.

[28:08] David Patel: Like a small script.

[28:10] Logan Wright: Exactly.

[28:12] Dylan Brooks: Another risk is missing stickiness key. We’ll add validation warnings in Console if the key is absent or low entropy.

[28:21] Sarah Kim: Good.

[28:23] Marissa Cole: Another risk is results freshness. We’ll set expectation on aggregation delays.

[28:29] Sarah Kim: Okay.

[28:31] Renee Zhao: My risk is beta features changing. Like config schema changes.

[28:36] Dylan Brooks: We version configs and provide migrations. But for POC, we’ll keep it stable. If we need to change, we’ll coordinate.

[28:46] Renee Zhao: Okay.

[28:48] Marissa Cole: Any procurement constraints.

[28:50] Sarah Kim: Not for POC. For production, yes.

[28:53] Marissa Cole: Great.

[28:55] David Patel: One more technical thing. Can we force the same prompt template across variants to compare. Like, ensure the request is identical.

[29:03] Dylan Brooks: Yes. Since routing happens before inference, the request payload can be identical. The only difference is which model is selected.

[29:11] David Patel: Great.

[29:13] Logan Wright: And we’ll attribute metrics based on the selected variant so you get clean breakdowns.

[29:19] Sarah Kim: Great.

[29:21] Marissa Cole: Okay. Next steps: I’ll send POC success criteria and beta enablement requirements by Thursday. Dylan sends example config and integration notes. Logan sends metric definitions and how cost is computed. Sarah, you’ll confirm routes and baseline QPS. Renee, you’ll send audit requirements.

[29:43] Sarah Kim: Yes.

[29:44] Renee Zhao: Yep.

[29:46] David Patel: Yep.

[29:47] Marissa Cole: And we’ll schedule a UI walkthrough early next week.

[29:52] Sarah Kim: Sounds good.

[29:54] Marissa Cole: Cool. Anything else before we drop.

[29:57] (silence)

[30:00] Marissa Cole: Great, thanks everyone.

---

Note: Transcript continues with light scheduling talk and calendar coordination until end of recording.

[30:12] Sarah Kim: Actually, sorry, just scheduling. Tuesday morning works.

[30:17] Marissa Cole: Perfect, I’ll send it.

[30:20] (call continues with scheduling details; no new technical content)

title: POC scoping - route experiments / A-B testing (Northbridge)
preview: Northbridge is scoping a POC to run route-level A/B tests between model variants (cost vs latency vs output quality) and wants first-class measurement in Redwood Console: p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate, throughput, and cost per request / cost per 1k tokens broken out by variant. They need deterministic assignment (stickiness) keyed off their end-user or account id, plus the ability to ramp traffic safely with guardrails (soft-stop initially ok, but they want an option for auto-stop for severe p99 regressions). They also raised enterprise requirements: RBAC on who can start/stop experiments, audit logging of config changes, and clarity on how experiment attribution shows up in logs/headers for debugging. Timeline: start POC in early March, run for ~2 weeks, decision by late March for an April/May rollout on a subset of routes.
text: Northbridge is scoping a POC to run route-level A/B tests between model variants (cost vs latency vs output quality) and wants first-class measurement in Redwood Console: p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate, throughput, and cost per request / cost per 1k tokens broken out by variant. They need deterministic assignment (stickiness) keyed off their end-user or account id, plus the ability to ramp traffic safely with guardrails (soft-stop initially ok, but they want an option for auto-stop for severe p99 regressions). They also raised enterprise requirements: RBAC on who can start/stop experiments, audit logging of config changes, and clarity on how experiment attribution shows up in logs/headers for debugging. Timeline: start POC in early March, run for ~2 weeks, decision by late March for an April/May rollout on a subset of routes.
